did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) for early prediction of melanoma from skin lesion images. Specifically, it evaluated a custom non-pretrained CNN and three pretrained models (MobileNetV2, EfficientNetV2, and DenseNet121) on the HAM10000 dataset to perform binary classification between melanoma and non-melanoma skin conditions.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the training and evaluation of CNN-based models (one non-pretrained and three pretrained: MobileNetV2, EfficientNetV2, DenseNet121) on the HAM10000 dataset for melanoma detection. It includes specific sections for Methods, Results, and Discussion, reporting new metrics such as accuracy, recall, and F1-score, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Training CNN-based Model on Low Resource Hardware and Small Dataset for Early Prediction of Melanoma from Skin Lesion Images
journal: JURNAL EMACS (Engineering, MAthematics and Computer Science)
brief_summary: This study aimed to determine the most suitable deep learning method for early melanoma prediction using limited data and low-resource hardware. The researchers evaluated one custom non-pretrained CNN and three pretrained models (MobileNetV2, EfficientNetV2, and DenseNet121) for binary classification of melanoma versus non-melanoma skin conditions. The models were trained and tested on the HAM10000 dataset, which was split into 60% training, 20% validation, and 20% test sets. Evaluation metrics included accuracy, recall, and F1-score, with recall prioritized for early detection purposes. The results demonstrated that pretrained models achieved better overall accuracy and recall compared to the non-pretrained model. The study concluded that pretrained models, particularly MobileNetV2 which achieved the highest recall, are more suitable for melanoma detection under constrained data and hardware resources.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary target class for the binary classification task. The study focuses on early prediction of melanoma. The HAM10000 dataset contained 8903 images of melanoma.

The negative class in the binary classification task. The study used the HAM10000 dataset which contains benign and malignant pigmented lesions, but the experiment specifically performed binary classification between melanoma and non-melanoma. The dataset includes classes such as actinic keratosis, basal cell carcinoma, benign keratosis, melanocytic nevi, dermatofibroma, and vascular lesions, but these were grouped into the 'non-melanoma' category for the experiment.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification between melanoma and non-melanoma skin conditions
all_task_types.details: The study performs binary classification to predict whether a skin lesion image contains melanoma or non-melanoma. The task is explicitly defined as 'binary classification between melanoma and non-melanoma skin conditions' using the HAM10000 dataset. Evaluation metrics include accuracy, recall, precision, and F1-score, with recall prioritized for early detection.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A large collection of multi-source dermatoscopic images of common pigmented skin lesions, collected from patients with severe chronic sun damage over a period of 20 years in Austria.

use.training: Used for training the CNN models (custom non-pretrained, MobileNetV2, EfficientNetV2, DenseNet121) after splitting into 60% for training.
use.validation: Used for validation after splitting into 20% of the dataset.
use.testing: Used for testing after splitting into 20% of the dataset.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Model performance was compared using descriptive statistics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score) and visual inspection of loss curves and confusion matrices.

HAM10000.notes: All models achieved above 90% accuracy and above 96% precision. None of the models achieved higher than 89% recall. MobileNetV2 achieved the highest accuracy (93.77%) and highest recall (89.78%). DenseNet121 achieved the highest precision (99.81%). Pretrained models showed smoother convergence and lower loss compared to the non-pretrained model. The study prioritized recall for early detection purposes.

architecture_used.main_model: Four CNN-based models: one custom non-pretrained CNN, and three pretrained models (MobileNetV2, EfficientNetV2, DenseNet121).
architecture_used.pretraining: MobileNetV2, EfficientNetV2, and DenseNet121 were pretrained on ImageNet (Deng et al., 2009). The custom CNN was trained from scratch.

layers: Four convolutional blocks, each consisting of a 3x3 kernel convolutional layer, ReLU activation, and 2x2 max pooling layer.
One block with a 3x3 kernel convolutional layer, ReLU activation, and global average pooling layer with 2x2 kernel.
Series of fully connected layers with dropout layers in between.

mobilenetv2.description: Pretrained ImageNet model designed for mobile devices.
features: Single stride block with residual connection
Dual stride block without residual connection

efficientnetv2.description: Pretrained ImageNet model optimized for faster training and reduced parameters using neural architecture search and progressive learning.

densenet121.description: Pretrained ImageNet model designed to solve vanishing gradient problems by densely connecting layers.
building_blocks: Dense blocks where each layer takes all preceding layers as input

classification_or_output_head.output_activation: Implied sigmoid for binary cross-entropy loss

preprocessing_techniques: Data augmentation on the training set including image rotation, horizontal image mirroring, and changing contrast
Conversion of all images to grayscale

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to the training set samples to generate more data.
Applied to the training set samples to generate more data.
Applied to the training set samples to generate more data.

conclusions: Pretrained models (MobileNetV2, EfficientNetV2, DenseNet121) achieved better overall accuracy and recall compared to the custom non-pretrained CNN model when using limited sample images and low hardware resources.

MobileNetV2 achieved the highest recall (89.78%) and highest accuracy (93.77%) among all tested models, making it the most suitable model for early melanoma detection under constrained data and hardware resources.
DenseNet121 achieved the highest precision (99.81%) among the pretrained models.

Pretrained models demonstrated smoother convergence and lower loss during training compared to the non-pretrained model, suggesting they can achieve lower loss with fewer epochs and reduced training time.
None of the models achieved a recall higher than 89%, indicating that further studies are necessary to reduce the number of false negatives for early melanoma detection.
The study's results were not tested on real-world patient data, posing a challenge as images taken from various devices may affect prediction results.
Training a model from scratch is less suitable than using transfer learning with pretrained models for this task given the small dataset size and hardware constraints.
important_remarks: The study did not test the models on real-world patient data, posing a challenge as images taken from various devices may affect prediction results.
None of the models achieved a recall higher than 89%, indicating a need for further studies to reduce false negatives for early melanoma detection.

The dataset (HAM10000) consists of images from patients with severe chronic sun damage over 20 years in Austria, potentially limiting generalizability to populations with different skin types or sun exposure histories.

The custom non-pretrained CNN showed significant loss fluctuation during training, likely due to the small batch size (32) and limited dataset, whereas pretrained models demonstrated smoother convergence.
The study prioritized recall as the most important metric for early detection, yet all models failed to achieve high recall (>89%), which is critical for minimizing missed melanoma cases.
The experiment was conducted on low-resource hardware constraints, which may limit the applicability of the findings to high-performance clinical settings where computational resources are abundant.
